payload.message: I have one useful signal already: on `edgesynology2`, `sdc` is `ata3.00` and it cleanly negotiates `6.0 Gbps`, with no obvious hard-reset or I/O-error storm in `dmesg`. I’m fixing the sampling commands now to get the per-sample `sda` numbers on `edgesynology1` and the per-device counters on `edgesynology2`.

content.text: I have one useful signal already: on `edgesynology2`, `sdc` is `ata3.00` and it cleanly negotiates `6.0 Gbps`, with no obvious hard-reset or I/O-error storm in `dmesg`. I’m fixing the sampling commands now to get the per-sample `sda` numbers on `edgesynology1` and the per-device counters on `edgesynology2`.

payload.message:
On `edgesynology1`, `sda` is not showing the same pathology as `edgesynology2`’s `sdc`.

From a 6-sample `iostat -x 1 6` run on May 1, 2026, `edgesynology1` `sda` looked roughly like this:
- throughput:
  - reads about `6.3 MB/s` to `21.3 MB/s`
  - writes about `12 KB/s` to `447 KB/s`
- latency:
  - read await about `8 ms` to `35 ms`
  - write await about `22 ms` to `82 ms`
- utilization:
  - about `56%` to `88%`

That is not amazing, but it is nowhere near `edgesynology2` `sdc`, which was hitting:
- `~97-100% util`
- read await up to `~959 ms`
- write await up to `~548 ms`

So the same 20TB model can behave much more normally on `edgesynology1`. That weakens the “20TB model/firmware alone is the root cause” theory.

On `edgesynology2`, the storage-path diagnosis is still local and still points at `sdc`, but with an important refinement:
- `sdc` is `ata3.00`
- it negotiates cleanly at `6.0 Gbps`
- SMART is clean
- `dmesg` does not show an obvious storm of hard resets, failed commands, or SATA link flaps for that drive

So this does not currently look like a classic dying-disk or bad-cable signature. It still looks like a local path/performance problem centered on that device under workload.

And yes, to be precise on your snapshot note: I understand you said the `SnapshotReplication` package is not running, not that snapshots disappeared. From what I checked:
- `SnapshotReplication` is stopped
- `ReplicationService` is running
- `ScsiTarget` is stopped

That does not, by itself, prove local snapshots are gone or dependent on iSCSI.

Current best diagnosis:
- `edgesynology2` has a real local storage bottleneck
- `sdc` is still the strongest live suspect
- but the evidence is performance asymmetry, not SMART failure or clear SATA errors

The next useful step is to inspect this in DSM on `edgesynology2` specifically for the `sdc` bay:
- drive-level response time/performance panel
- controller/bay warnings
- storage pool disk performance view

If you want, I can keep going from SSH and try to extract Synology-specific disk/bay mapping so we can identify exactly which physical slot corresponds to `sdc`.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

payload.message: I found Synology’s disk-latency database and health JSON. I’m reading those directly now, which should give a more authoritative view than `iostat` alone.

content.text: I found Synology’s disk-latency database and health JSON. I’m reading those directly now, which should give a more authoritative view than `iostat` alone.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$p='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; $cmd=@"
printf 'D@Mp0p123\n' | sudo -S file /var/log/disk-latency/.SYNODISKLATENCYDB 2>/dev/null
echo __SEP__
printf 'D@Mp0p123\n' | sudo -S sqlite3 /var/log/disk-latency/.SYNODISKLATENCYDB '.tables' 2>/dev/null
echo __SEP__
printf 'D@Mp0p123\n' | sudo -S sqlite3 /var/log/disk-latency/.SYNODISKLATENCYDB '.schema' 2>/dev/null | sed -n '1,220p'

payload.message:
Two concrete findings.

First, `edgesynology1` `sda` does not look like `edgesynology2` `sdc`. In a fresh 6-sample run, `edgesynology1` `sda` was busy but not pathological:
- read throughput roughly `6.3` to `21.3 MB/s`
- write throughput roughly `0.01` to `0.45 MB/s`
- read await roughly `8` to `35 ms`
- write await roughly `22` to `82 ms`
- util roughly `57%` to `88%`

That is materially better than `edgesynology2` `sdc`, which was repeatedly showing hundreds of milliseconds of await and `~100% util`. So the same general drive family is not enough to explain the problem.

Second, I now have the physical mapping for `edgesynology2` from Synology’s own disk-latency DB. `sdc` is bay `3`.

Current `edgesynology2` mapping:
- bay `1` = `sda` = serial `TEG4TX4Z` = `WD260KFGX-68CNGN0`
- bay `2` = `sdb` = serial `TAG1VKKH` = `WUH722626ALE6L4`
- bay `3` = `sdc` = serial `2LGDTR6F` = `WUH722020ALE6L4`
- bay `4` = `sdd` = serial `3JG4WNVG` = `WUH721818ALE6L4`
- bay `5` = `sde` = serial `3RK881WA` = `WUH721818ALE604`

That same Synology latency DB also shows `sdc` as the clear outlier relative to the other four disks. I can’t fully decode every internal field because Synology doesn’t document that schema, but the relative differences are strong:

- `sdc` has much lower throughput/IO progress counters than peers in several fields.
- `sdc` has a very large outlier value where peers cluster tightly:
  - field `31`: `4.42` on `sdc` vs about `0.49` to `0.62` on others
  - field `32`: `80.18` on `sdc` vs about `16` to `22` on others
- `sdc` also has much larger event/count totals in fields `20` and `27` than peers.

So the box is telling the same story in three independent ways:
- Linux `iostat`
- live runtime behavior
- Synology’s own latency database

What does not fit a classic hardware-failure signature:
- SMART is clean
- no SATA link flaps
- no failed-command storm in `dmesg`
- `ata3.00` links at `6.0 Gbps` cleanly

So the working diagnosis is now tighter:

- the problem is still local to `edgesynology2`
- it is centered on bay `3` / `sdc`
- but it looks more like a severe performance-path issue than a conventional SMART-detectable disk failure

The next practical move is physical and targeted:
- inspect bay `3`
- reseat that drive first
- if you can schedule it safely, move that exact drive to another bay and see whether the problem follows the drive or stays with bay `3`

That is now the cleanest discriminator:
- if the problem follows the drive, it is the disk despite clean SMART
- if it stays with bay `3`, it is the bay/backplane/controller path

If you want, I can next give you a very explicit low-risk test sequence for reseat/swap on `edgesynology2` based on this mapping.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

PKG_DIR="/var/packages/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}"
PKG_APP_LINK_PATH="/usr/syno/synoman/webman/3rdparty/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}"
SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER="/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/updater"

parsed_cmd.cmd:
$p='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; $cmd=@"

payload.aggregated_output:
#!/bin/bash
# Copyright (c) 2000-2019 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

. /var/packages/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}/scripts/pkg_utils

start()
{
	# If in the SHA safe mode
	if /usr/syno/sbin/synohacore --is_ha_running; then
		if /usr/local/bin/synoha --is-safemode; then
			return 0
		fi
	fi

	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "webapi_ready"
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl start pkg-iscsi
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		return 1
	fi
	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "service_ready"
	return $?
}

stop()
{
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl stop pkg-iscsi
	return $?
}

status()
{
	if [ -e ${PKG_APP_LINK_PATH} ]; then
		return 0
	else
		return 1
	fi
}

case $1 in
	start)
		start
		exit $?
	;;
	stop)
		stop
		exit 0
	;;
	status)
		status
		exit $?
	;;
	killall)
	;;
	log)
		exit 0
	;;
esac

exit 0

#!/bin/bash
# Copyright (c) 2000-2019 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

. /var/packages/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}/scripts/pkg_utils

start()
{
	# If in the SHA safe mode
	if /usr/syno/sbin/synohacore --is_ha_running; then
		if /usr/local/bin/synoha --is-safemode; then
			return 0
		fi
	fi

	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "webapi_ready"
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl start pkg-iscsi
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		return 1
	fi
	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "service_ready"
	return $?
}

stop()
{
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl stop pkg-iscsi
	return $?
}

status()
{
	if [ -e ${PKG_APP_LINK_PATH} ]; then
		return 0
	else
		return 1
	fi
}

case $1 in
	start)
		start
		exit $?
	;;
	stop)
		stop
		exit 0
	;;
	status)
		status
		exit $?
	;;
	killall)
	;;
	log)
		exit 0
	;;
esac

exit 0

content.text:
connect to edgesynology2 via SSH:
SSH popdam@192.168.3.101 port 1904
password: D@Mp0p123

SSH access to `edgesynology2` at `192.168.3.101:1904` is working. I verified it by running `uname -a`, which returned:

`Linux edgesynology2 4.4.302+ ... x86_64 GNU/Linux synology_broadwellnk_1621xs+`

If you want, I can now run commands on that host for you.

another ai session cam to the following conclusions, do you agree:
edgesynology2 Full Diagnostic Report
🔴 CRITICAL — The [6/5] Array Problem (md0 & md1)
What it is: The system RAID arrays md0 and md1 (the boot/system partitions, not your data) are configured to expect 6 drives but only see 5. That's why DSM reports "6 disks" — it's the ghost slot of a drive that used to be in the array's metadata but was never cleaned up.
What it looks like: [6/5] [UUUUU_] — 5 active members out of an expected 6, with one empty slot (_).
Is your data at risk? No. These are the tiny system boot partitions (md0 = 2.4GB, md1 = 2GB), not your data. Your data arrays (md2 through md6) all show a clean [5/5] [UUUUU]. The data is fine.
Why it happened: Almost certainly a drive was replaced or swapped at some point. The old drive's slot was never removed from the array metadata. It's been sitting like this probably for a long time.

🔴 CRITICAL — iSCSI rodsp Login Storm
What it is: The kernel log shows continuous iSCSI login failures firing roughly every 5 seconds, 24/7, since at least April 6th (25 days ago). The error rodsp_remote_client_login_init failed, ret=-22 means something is repeatedly trying to connect to an iSCSI target on this NAS and failing every single time — but never giving up.
Why it matters: This is generating constant kernel-level interrupts and I/O context switching. It's a significant contributor to your CPU iowait. There's no iSCSI package installed, no active iSCSI targets configured — so whatever is hammering it is hitting a broken/unconfigured service. This should be completely silenced.
What's causing it: Most likely edgesynology1 (your other NAS) is configured to access an iSCSI LUN on edgesynology2 that no longer exists or was never properly set up.

🔴 CRITICAL — CPU iowait at 36%
Normal is under 5%. You're at 36%. This means 36% of the time, the CPU is doing nothing but waiting for disk operations to complete. The system had a major I/O freeze event on April 30 at 12:35 AM where dockerd, the DSM API, the backup daemon, cloud-worker, and the agent were all blocked for 120+ seconds.
Root causes, in order:

md2 RAID array has 728 I/O operations queued — wildly high. Normal is 0–5. This is the primary bottleneck. dm-1 and dm-2 (the device mapper layer on top of it) show 743 queued ops.
sdc disk has 130 queued I/O operations — sdc is the 20TB drive (different size from your 4 other drives). It's the slowest member of the RAID5 arrays and is dragging everything down.
btrfs-cleaner has been stuck in D-state (disk wait) since April 6th — 25 days. It's been continuously trying to do Btrfs maintenance and getting stuck behind the I/O queue.
jbd2/md0-8 also stuck in D-state since April 6th — the journal for the system partition has been hung for 25 days.


🟠 SERIOUS — Btrfs Corruption Errors
25 corruption errors recorded on the main volume (/dev/mapper/cachedev_0). Write/read I/O errors are zero, which is good — the drives themselves are healthy (all 5 SMART statuses are OK). But the Btrfs filesystem layer has detected 25 data corruption events. These could be caused by the I/O storm above (incomplete writes during freezes).
Also: btrfs fi usage is throwing ERROR: unexpected number of devices: 0 != 1 — the Btrfs volume is in a confused state about its own device count.

🟠 SERIOUS — CloudSync Running for 25 Days Straight, Burning CPU
Process syno-cloud-syncd (PID 30200) has been running continuously for 25+ days and has consumed 3,124 hours of CPU time (that's not a typo — over 3,000 hours). It's currently the #1 CPU consumer at 8.5%. This process is CloudSync (Synology's Google Drive / Dropbox / OneDrive sync). It appears to be stuck in a sync loop — possibly because the volume it's syncing to/from is backed up with I/O, so it never finishes, so it never stops.

🟡 NOTABLE — Swap Usage (1.4GB)
With 31GB RAM and 28GB actually available, you shouldn't be using any swap at all. The 1.4GB of swap in use suggests some processes were swapped out during one of those I/O freeze events and never reclaimed. Not critical but worth noting.

🟡 NOTABLE — avahi-daemon burning 790 hours of CPU
The network discovery daemon (avahi) has consumed 790 hours of CPU. It's a low-priority process but shouldn't need that much. This may be related to the constant iSCSI connection noise confusing the network layer.

Summary & Recommended Fix Order
PriorityIssueFix1iSCSI login stormDisable iSCSI initiator on edgesynology1, or disable iSCSI Manager on edgesynology22md0/md1 ghost 6th disk slotRemove the missing slot from array metadata3CloudSync stuck loopStop and restart CloudSync; investigate what it's syncing4Btrfs corruption errorsRun a Btrfs scrub after fixing I/O5btrfs-cleaner / jbd2 stuck processesWill resolve once I/O pressure drops

The md0/md1 issue is not the first thing I would touch now. Since this is a `DS1621xs+` class 6-bay unit and you say bay 6 was never populated, `[6/5] [UUUUU_]` is more likely “array geometry tracks chassis capacity” than evidence of a removed historical disk. Your `mdadm --remove detached` commands did nothing because there was no detached member to remove; `mdadm --detail` already shows slot 5 as plain `removed` and `Failed Devices : 0`. Changing that would mean reshaping the array from 6 raid devices to 5, which is not where I’d spend risk budget while the box still has active I/O problems.

The first remediation target is iSCSI. `ScsiTarget` is installed and running, TCP `3261` is listening, and the kernel is logging repeated `rodsp_remote_client_login_init ... ret=-22` failures. Since you do not use SAN/iSCSI, stopping that package is the cleanest first test because it is low-risk and directly matches a confirmed fault pattern.

Recommended order:

1. Stop SAN Manager / iSCSI target service.
   ```sh
   printf 'D@Mp0p123\n' | sudo -S /usr/syno/bin/synopkg stop ScsiTarget
   ```
   Then confirm the login storm stops:
   ```sh
   dmesg | grep -i rodsp | tail -n 20
   netstat -lntp 2>/dev/null | grep 3261
   ```
   If nothing breaks and you truly do not use it, leave it stopped and disable it in DSM Package Center or uninstall it later.

2. Reduce sync pressure after iSCSI is quiet.
   The heavy long-running sync processes on this NAS are from `SynologyDrive` / `ShareSync`, not just generic “Cloud Sync.” If this NAS can tolerate a temporary pause, stop Synology Drive first:
   ```sh
   printf 'D@Mp0p123\n' | sudo -S /usr/syno/bin/synopkg stop SynologyDrive
   ```
   If you also actively use the `CloudSync` package, stop that separately in DSM or with `synopkg stop CloudSync`. Do this only if pausing sync jobs is acceptable.

3. Recheck I/O after those two reductions.
   ```sh
   top -b -n 1 | head -n 20
   iostat -x 1 3
   ps -eo pid,stat,comm,wchan:32,etime | grep ' D '
   ```
   The goal is to see whether `iowait`, `dm-1/dm-2` saturation, and long-lived D-state activity drop materially.

4. Only after the box is calmer, run a filesystem scrub.
   There is real Btrfs evidence of `corrupt 25` on `cachedev_0`, but not active read/write error counters. A scrub is appropriate after load is reduced, not before. Prefer doing this from DSM Storage Manager so the status is visible there.

5. Leave md0/md1 alone for now.
   It is not the current performance bottleneck, and your data arrays are healthy. If the warning persists after the box is otherwise healthy and you still want to normalize it, that becomes a separate, deliberate Synology-specific maintenance task.

6. Ignore `avahi` for now.
   It is not a top-tier problem compared to iSCSI noise, sync load, and storage-path saturation.

- `edgesynology2` has a real local storage bottleneck

- but the evidence is performance asymmetry, not SMART failure or clear SATA errors

Two concrete findings.

First, `edgesynology1` `sda` does not look like `edgesynology2` `sdc`. In a fresh 6-sample run, `edgesynology1` `sda` was busy but not pathological:
- read throughput roughly `6.3` to `21.3 MB/s`
- write throughput roughly `0.01` to `0.45 MB/s`
- read await roughly `8` to `35 ms`
- write await roughly `22` to `82 ms`
- util roughly `57%` to `88%`

That is materially better than `edgesynology2` `sdc`, which was repeatedly showing hundreds of milliseconds of await and `~100% util`. So the same general drive family is not enough to explain the problem.

Second, I now have the physical mapping for `edgesynology2` from Synology’s own disk-latency DB. `sdc` is bay `3`.

Current `edgesynology2` mapping:
- bay `1` = `sda` = serial `TEG4TX4Z` = `WD260KFGX-68CNGN0`
- bay `2` = `sdb` = serial `TAG1VKKH` = `WUH722626ALE6L4`
- bay `3` = `sdc` = serial `2LGDTR6F` = `WUH722020ALE6L4`
- bay `4` = `sdd` = serial `3JG4WNVG` = `WUH721818ALE6L4`
- bay `5` = `sde` = serial `3RK881WA` = `WUH721818ALE604`

That same Synology latency DB also shows `sdc` as the clear outlier relative to the other four disks. I can’t fully decode every internal field because Synology doesn’t document that schema, but the relative differences are strong:

- `sdc` has much lower throughput/IO progress counters than peers in several fields.
- `sdc` has a very large outlier value where peers cluster tightly:
  - field `31`: `4.42` on `sdc` vs about `0.49` to `0.62` on others
  - field `32`: `80.18` on `sdc` vs about `16` to `22` on others
- `sdc` also has much larger event/count totals in fields `20` and `27` than peers.

So the box is telling the same story in three independent ways:
- Linux `iostat`
- live runtime behavior
- Synology’s own latency database

What does not fit a classic hardware-failure signature:
- SMART is clean
- no SATA link flaps
- no failed-command storm in `dmesg`
- `ata3.00` links at `6.0 Gbps` cleanly

So the working diagnosis is now tighter:

- the problem is still local to `edgesynology2`
- it is centered on bay `3` / `sdc`
- but it looks more like a severe performance-path issue than a conventional SMART-detectable disk failure

The next practical move is physical and targeted:
- inspect bay `3`
- reseat that drive first
- if you can schedule it safely, move that exact drive to another bay and see whether the problem follows the drive or stays with bay `3`

That is now the cleanest discriminator:
- if the problem follows the drive, it is the disk despite clean SMART
- if it stays with bay `3`, it is the bay/backplane/controller path

If you want, I can next give you a very explicit low-risk test sequence for reseat/swap on `edgesynology2` based on this mapping.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

payload.message: The one-liners are fighting Synology’s shell quoting. I’m switching to a temp command file and `plink -m` so I can inspect the actual iSCSI config cleanly.

content.text: The one-liners are fighting Synology’s shell quoting. I’m switching to a temp command file and `plink -m` so I can inspect the actual iSCSI config cleanly.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
ls -1 /usr/syno/etc 2>/dev/null | grep ^iscsi_ || true
echo ---
ls -1 /usr/syno/etc.defaults 2>/dev/null | grep ^iscsi_ || true
echo ---
for f in /usr/syno/etc/iscsi_* /usr/syno/etc.defaults/iscsi_*; do
  if [ -f "$f" ]; then
    echo "=== $f ==="
    sed -n "1,220p" "$f"
  fi
done
'
'@; $tmp=Join-Path $env:TEMP 'edgesynology2-iscsi-read.txt'; Set-Content -LiteralPath $tmp -Value $script -NoNewline; $plink='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; & $plink -batch -P 1904 -pw 'D@Mp0p123' -m $tmp popdam@192.168.3.101","workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\Codex\2026-05-01\connect-to-edgesynology2-via-ssh-ssh","timeout_ms":40000}

#!/bin/bash
# Copyright (c) 2000-2019 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

. /var/packages/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}/scripts/pkg_utils

start()
{
	# If in the SHA safe mode
	if /usr/syno/sbin/synohacore --is_ha_running; then
		if /usr/local/bin/synoha --is-safemode; then
			return 0
		fi
	fi

	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "webapi_ready"
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl start pkg-iscsi
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		return 1
	fi
	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "service_ready"
	return $?
}

stop()
{
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl stop pkg-iscsi
	return $?
}

status()
{
	if [ -e ${PKG_APP_LINK_PATH} ]; then
		return 0
	else
		return 1
	fi
}

case $1 in
	start)
		start
		exit $?
	;;
	stop)
		stop
		exit 0
	;;
	status)
		status
		exit $?
	;;
	killall)
	;;
	log)
		exit 0
	;;
esac

exit 0

#!/bin/sh
# Copyright (c) 2000-2016 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

ISCSITRGT_LOCK="/tmp/S78iscsitrg.lock"

. /etc.defaults/rc.subr

lio_kernel_module_status()
{
	/sbin/lsmod | /bin/grep -q iscsi_target_mod
	if [ "$?" -eq "0" ]; then
		echo "0"
	else
		echo "-1"
	fi
}

## iSCSI target rc generic interface
iscsitrg_start()
{
	/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synocheckiscsitrg > /dev/null 2>&1
	if [ "$(lio_kernel_module_status)" -ne "0" ]; then
		RCMsg "Running lunbackup garbage collection" \
		/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synolunbkp --gc

		mods=("iscsi" "vhost-scsi" "tcm" "loopback" "fc")
		for mod in "${mods[@]}"
		do
			/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synoiscsiep --startall "$mod" > /dev/null 2>&1
		done
	fi
}

iscsitrg_stop()
{
	/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synoiscsiep --oil
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		MsgFail "Stopping iSCSI modules"
	else
		# check the consistent between files and configs
		/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synocheckiscsitrg > /dev/null 2>&1
	fi
}

iscsitrg_restart()
{
	iscsitrg_stop
	iscsitrg_start
}

check_support()
{
	local iscsitrg_support=`/bin/get_key_value /etc.defaults/synoinfo.conf support_iscsi_target`
	case "${iscsitrg_support}" in
		[Yy][Ee][Ss])
			return $LSB_SUCCESS
			;;
		*)
			RCMsg "iSCSI target is not supported."
			return $LSB_ERR_CONFIGURED
			;;
	esac
}

iscsitrg_unlock()
{
	/bin/rm "${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}"
}

iscsitrg_lock_trap_unset()
{
	trap - INT TERM EXIT ABRT
}

iscsitrg_lock_trap_set()
{
	trap 'iscsitrg_unlock' INT TERM EXIT ABRT
}

iscsitrg_lock()
{
	MAX_RETRY_COUNT=10
	retryCount=0

	while [ $retryCount -lt $MAX_RETRY_COUNT ]; do
		if [ -f ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK} ]; then
			local script_pid="`cat ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}`"

			kill -0 $script_pid
			if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
				MsgWarn "$0 (${script_pid}: \"`ps --no-headers -o comm ${script_pid}`\") is running. retry: ($retryCount / $MAX_RETRY_COUNT)"
			else
				MsgWarn "$0 ($script_pid) is NOT running."
				break
			fi
		else
			break
		fi

		sleep 3
		retryCount=$((retryCount + 1))
	done
	if [ $retryCount -eq $MAX_RETRY_COUNT ]; then
		MsgWarn "$0 Reach max retry count. exit!"
		return 1
	fi

	iscsitrg_lock_trap_set

	echo $$ > ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}
	return 0
}

[ "$#" -ne "0" ] && ! check_support && exit $?

iscsitrg_lock

lock_result=$?
[ "${lock_result}" -eq "1" ] && exit $LSB_STAT_RUNNING

case $1 in
	start)
		iscsitrg_start
		;;
	stop)
		iscsitrg_stop
		;;
	restart)
		iscsitrg_restart
		;;
	*)
		echo "Usage: `/usr/bin/basename $0` {start|stop|restart|status}"
		exit 1
esac

exit $?

=== /var/packages/ScsiTarget/scripts/start-stop-status ===
#!/bin/bash
# Copyright (c) 2000-2019 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

. /var/packages/${SYNOPKG_PKGNAME}/scripts/pkg_utils

start()
{
	# If in the SHA safe mode
	if /usr/syno/sbin/synohacore --is_ha_running; then
		if /usr/local/bin/synoha --is-safemode; then
			return 0
		fi
	fi

	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "webapi_ready"
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl start pkg-iscsi
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		return 1
	fi
	${SYNOSCSITPKG_UPDATER} "service_ready"
	return $?
}

stop()
{
	/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl stop pkg-iscsi
	return $?
}

status()
{
	if [ -e ${PKG_APP_LINK_PATH} ]; then
		return 0
	else
		return 1
	fi
}

case $1 in
	start)
		start
		exit $?
	;;
	stop)
		stop
		exit 0
	;;
	status)
		status
		exit $?
	;;
	killall)
	;;
	log)
		exit 0
	;;
esac

exit 0

=== /var/packages/ScsiTarget/scripts/S78iscsitrg.sh ===
#!/bin/sh
# Copyright (c) 2000-2016 Synology Inc. All rights reserved.

ISCSITRGT_LOCK="/tmp/S78iscsitrg.lock"

. /etc.defaults/rc.subr

lio_kernel_module_status()
{
	/sbin/lsmod | /bin/grep -q iscsi_target_mod
	if [ "$?" -eq "0" ]; then
		echo "0"
	else
		echo "-1"
	fi
}

## iSCSI target rc generic interface
iscsitrg_start()
{
	/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synocheckiscsitrg > /dev/null 2>&1
	if [ "$(lio_kernel_module_status)" -ne "0" ]; then
		RCMsg "Running lunbackup garbage collection" \
		/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synolunbkp --gc

		mods=("iscsi" "vhost-scsi" "tcm" "loopback" "fc")
		for mod in "${mods[@]}"
		do
			/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synoiscsiep --startall "$mod" > /dev/null 2>&1
		done
	fi
}

iscsitrg_stop()
{
	/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synoiscsiep --oil
	if [ "$?" -ne "0" ]; then
		MsgFail "Stopping iSCSI modules"
	else
		# check the consistent between files and configs
		/var/packages/ScsiTarget/target/bin/synocheckiscsitrg > /dev/null 2>&1
	fi
}

iscsitrg_restart()
{
	iscsitrg_stop
	iscsitrg_start
}

check_support()
{
	local iscsitrg_support=`/bin/get_key_value /etc.defaults/synoinfo.conf support_iscsi_target`
	case "${iscsitrg_support}" in
		[Yy][Ee][Ss])
			return $LSB_SUCCESS
			;;
		*)
			RCMsg "iSCSI target is not supported."
			return $LSB_ERR_CONFIGURED
			;;
	esac
}

iscsitrg_unlock()
{
	/bin/rm "${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}"
}

iscsitrg_lock_trap_unset()
{
	trap - INT TERM EXIT ABRT
}

iscsitrg_lock_trap_set()
{
	trap 'iscsitrg_unlock' INT TERM EXIT ABRT
}

iscsitrg_lock()
{
	MAX_RETRY_COUNT=10
	retryCount=0

	while [ $retryCount -lt $MAX_RETRY_COUNT ]; do
		if [ -f ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK} ]; then
			local script_pid="`cat ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}`"

			kill -0 $script_pid
			if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
				MsgWarn "$0 (${script_pid}: \"`ps --no-headers -o comm ${script_pid}`\") is running. retry: ($retryCount / $MAX_RETRY_COUNT)"
			else
				MsgWarn "$0 ($script_pid) is NOT running."
				break
			fi
		else
			break
		fi

		sleep 3
		retryCount=$((retryCount + 1))
	done
	if [ $retryCount -eq $MAX_RETRY_COUNT ]; then
		MsgWarn "$0 Reach max retry count. exit!"
		return 1
	fi

	iscsitrg_lock_trap_set

	echo $$ > ${ISCSITRGT_LOCK}
	return 0
}

[ "$#" -ne "0" ] && ! check_support && exit $?

iscsitrg_lock

lock_result=$?
[ "${lock_result}" -eq "1" ] && exit $LSB_STAT_RUNNING

case $1 in
	start)
		iscsitrg_start
		;;
	stop)
		iscsitrg_stop
		;;
	restart)
		iscsitrg_restart
		;;
	*)
		echo "Usage: `/usr/bin/basename $0` {start|stop|restart|status}"
		exit 1
esac

exit $?

	                            [allow_bkpobj] [storage_manager] [flashcache_status] [sync_progress] [family_config]
	                            [lun_type] [location] [category] [lun_types...]
	lun get(g)                  <uuid> [is_action_locked] [is_mapped] [extent_size] [allocated_size] [status] 
	                            [import_status] [sync_progress] [is_vhost_mapped] [is_bound] [whitelist]
	                            [flashcache_status] [family_config]
	lun set(s)                  <uuid> [new_name] [new_size] [new_type] [new_location] [vpd_unit_sn] [is_soft_feas_ignored] [dev_attribs ...]
	lun clone(cl)               <src_lun_uuid> <dst_lun_name> [clone_type] [dst_location]
	                            [dst_node_uuid] [dst_address] [dst_port]
	                            [is_data_encrypted] [is_soft_feas_ignored] [is_data_clone] [vpd_unit_sn]
	                            (is_OOO_XXX: true or false)
	lun map_target(mt)          <uuid> <target_ids ...>
	lun unmap_target(ut)        <uuid> <target_ids ...>
	lun load_lun(ll)            <uuid> [is_for_trash] [is_for_unsync_size]
	lun unload_lun(ul)          <uuid> [is_for_trash] [is_for_unsync_size]
	lun load_snapshot(ln)       <snapshot_uuid> [is_for_trash] [is_for_unsync_size]
	lun unload_snapshot(un)     <snapshot_uuid> [is_for_trash] [is_for_unsync_size]
	lun take_snapshot(ts)       <src_lun_uuid> <is_app_consistent> <is_locked> [snapshot_name] [description] [taken_by]
	                            [snapshot_time] [is_scheduled] [src_snap_dir] [snap_version] (is_OOXX: true or false)
	lun stop_snapshot(ss)       <src_lun_uuid> <snapshot_uuid>
	lun delete_snapshot(ds)     <snapshot_uuid> [deleted_by] [snapshot_uuids ...]
	lun list_snapshot(ls)       <src_lun_uuid> [is_count_only] [is_action_locked] [locked_app_key] [is_worm_locked]
	lun get_snapshot(gs)        <snapshot_uuid> [is_action_locked] [locked_app_key] [export_status] [sync_progress] [is_worm_locked]
	lun set_snapshot(es)        <snapshot_uuid> [snapshot_name] [is_user_locked] [description]
	lun restore_snapshot(rs)    <src_lun_uuid> <snapshot_uuid>
	lun clone_snapshot(cs)      <src_lun_uuid> <snapshot_uuid> <cloned_lun_name> [clone_type] [vpd_unit_sn]
	lun lock_snapshot(os)       <snapshot_uuid> <app_key>
	lun unlock_snapshot(us)     <snapshot_uuid> <app_key>
	lun export(ex)              <snapshot_uuid> <exported_dir_path>
	lun import(im)              <src_lun_uuid> <exported_dir_path> [snapshot_name] [description] [taken_by]
	lun export_cancel(ec)       <snapshot_uuid>
	lun import_cancel(ic)       <src_lun_uuid>
	lun map_vhost(mv)           <src_lun_uuid> [rod_key] [network_interface_name] [portals ...]
	lun unmap_vhost(uv)         <src_lun_uuid> [portals ...]
	lun loop_mount(lm)          <uuid>
	lun loop_unmount(lu)        <uuid>
	lun retry_stop(rst)         <uuid>
	lun set_whitelist(sw)       <uuid> <whitelist ...>
	lun stop_clone(sc)          <src_lun_uuid>
	lun defrag(df)              <uuid> [background] (background: true or false, default=true)
	lun stop_defrag(sdf)        <uuid>
	lun top(t)                  [object] [<attr> <value>] [lun_uuids ...] (object: true / false, default=false)
	lun acl_masks_add(aa)    <uuid> <{iqn  permission} ...>
	lun acl_masks_remove(ar) <uuid> <initiator_iqns ...>

Target(trg or t):
	target create(c)            <name> <iqn> <auth_type> [max_sessions] [user] [password] [mutual_user] [mutual_password] [is_soft_feas_ignored]
	                            (auth_type: 0: none, 1: single chap, 2: mutual chap)
	target delete(d)            <target_id>
	target list(l)              [lun_uuid] [mapped_lun] [acls] [connected_sessions] [status]
	target get(d)               <target_id> [mapped_lun] [acls] [connected_sessions] [status]
	target set(s)               <target_id> [-n name] [-i iqn] [-m max_sessions] 
	                            [-mr max_recv_seg_bytes] [-ms max_send_seg_bytes] 
	                            [-hh has_header_checksum] [-hd has_data_checksum] 
	                            [-c  auth_type user password mutual_user mutual_password]
	target enable(ea)           <target_id>
	target disable(da)          <target_id>
	target map_lun(ml)          <target_id> <lun_uuids ...>
	target unmap_lun(ul)        <target_id> <lun_uuids ...>
	target acl_masks_add(aa)    <target_id> <{iqn  permission} ...>
	target acl_masks_remove(ar) <target_id> <initiator_iqns ...>
	target network_portals_add(na)    <target_id> <network_portals ...> (ex:1.2.3.4:999)
	target network_portals_remove(nr) <target_id> <network_portals ...>

Replication(rep or r):
	rep create(c)               <src_lun_uuid> <dst_node_uuid> <dst_location> <dst_lun_name>
	rep delete(d)               <uuid> [delete_lun] (delete_lun: true or false)
	rep list(l)                 [lun_uuid]
Password:

payload.message: I have Synology’s own iSCSI CLI now. Next I’m using that to prove whether any targets or LUNs actually exist, because if they don’t, we can close the network exposure while leaving the SAN package itself installed and available.

payload.message: There are zero iSCSI targets, zero LUNs, and zero replication tasks on `edgesynology2`. That’s enough evidence to treat any inbound `3261` traffic as unwanted, so I’m checking the Synology firewall path next instead of touching storage or snapshot internals.

content.text: There are zero iSCSI targets, zero LUNs, and zero replication tasks on `edgesynology2`. That’s enough evidence to treat any inbound `3261` traffic as unwanted, so I’m checking the Synology firewall path next instead of touching storage or snapshot internals.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
which synofirewall 2>/dev/null || true
which nft 2>/dev/null || true
which iptables 2>/dev/null || true
echo ---
/usr/syno/bin/synosystemctl list-units | grep -Ei "firewall|nft|iptables" || true
echo ---
iptables -L -n -v 2>&1 | sed -n "1,120p"
echo ---
nft list ruleset 2>&1 | sed -n "1,200p"
'
'@; $tmp=Join-Path $env:TEMP 'edgesynology2-firewall-check.txt'; Set-Content -LiteralPath $tmp -Value $script -NoNewline; $plink='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; & $plink -batch -P 1904 -pw 'D@Mp0p123' -m $tmp popdam@192.168.3.101","workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\Codex\2026-05-01\connect-to-edgesynology2-via-ssh-ssh","timeout_ms":30000}

<environment_context>
  <shell>powershell</shell>
  <current_date>2026-05-03</current_date>

</environment_context>

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

content.text: continue

payload.message: continue

I have enough to make a narrow fix now: `edgesynology2` has no iSCSI targets, no LUNs, and no replication tasks, but `ScsiTarget` still exposes `3261`, which is the port tied to the `rodsp` failures. I’m blocking only inbound `3261` while leaving the package itself running, then I’ll verify from another NAS that the port is no longer reachable.

content.text: I have enough to make a narrow fix now: `edgesynology2` has no iSCSI targets, no LUNs, and no replication tasks, but `ScsiTarget` still exposes `3261`, which is the port tied to the `rodsp` failures. I’m blocking only inbound `3261` while leaving the package itself running, then I’ll verify from another NAS that the port is no longer reachable.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
iptables -C INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || iptables -I INPUT 1 -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP
ip6tables -C INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || ip6tables -I INPUT 1 -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP
iptables -L INPUT -n -v | sed -n "1,20p"
echo ---
ip6tables -L INPUT -n -v | sed -n "1,20p"
'
'@; $tmp=Join-Path $env:TEMP 'edgesynology2-block-3261.txt'; Set-Content -LiteralPath $tmp -Value $script -NoNewline; $plink='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; & $plink -batch -P 1904 -pw 'D@Mp0p123' -m $tmp popdam@192.168.3.101","workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\Codex\2026-05-01\connect-to-edgesynology2-via-ssh-ssh","timeout_ms":30000}

fi

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
cat > /usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh <<"EOF"
#!/bin/sh
case "$1" in
  start)
    /usr/bin/iptables -C DEFAULT_INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || /usr/bin/iptables -I DEFAULT_INPUT 1 -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP
    /usr/bin/ip6tables -C DEFAULT_INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || /usr/bin/ip6tables -I DEFAULT_INPUT 1 -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP
    ;;
  stop)
    /usr/bin/iptables -D DEFAULT_INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || true
    /usr/bin/ip6tables -D DEFAULT_INPUT -p tcp --dport 3261 -j DROP 2>/dev/null || true
    ;;
esac
exit 0
EOF
chmod 755 /usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh
/usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh start
sed -n "1,200p" /usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh
'
'@; $tmp=Join-Path $env:TEMP 'edgesynology2-persist-3261.txt'; Set-Content -LiteralPath $tmp -Value $script -NoNewline; $plink='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; & $plink -batch -P 1904 -pw 'D@Mp0p123' -m $tmp popdam@192.168.3.101","workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\Codex\2026-05-01\connect-to-edgesynology2-via-ssh-ssh","timeout_ms":30000}

payload.message:
I solved the iSCSI symptom without stopping the SAN package.

On `edgesynology2`, `ScsiTarget` is still running, and I confirmed there are still `0` iSCSI targets, `0` LUNs, and `0` replication tasks. The actual problem was that the package was still exposing TCP `3261`, which is the `rodsp`/LUN-replication path that had been generating the login-failure storm.

I blocked only inbound TCP `3261` on `edgesynology2` and made that persistent across reboot with `/usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh`. I verified the block from `edgesynology1`: the connection attempt now fails, and the `DEFAULT_INPUT` firewall counter on `edgesynology2` increments on the drop rule. `ScsiTarget` remains `running`, and `dmesg` is clean apart from the normal startup lines.

Rollback, if you ever actually need that port later:
- `sudo /usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh stop`
- remove `/usr/local/etc/rc.d/S99block_iscsi3261.sh`

This fixes the unwanted iSCSI ingress path while leaving the package and snapshot-related internals up. Next I’d return to the storage-path problem on `edgesynology2`, which is separate from this.

I solved the iSCSI symptom without stopping the SAN package.

On `edgesynology2`, `ScsiTarget` is still running, and I confirmed there are still `0` iSCSI targets, `0` LUNs, and `0` replication tasks. The actual problem was that the package was still exposing TCP `3261`, which is the `rodsp`/LUN-replication path that had been generating the login-failure storm.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
echo "=== host ==="
uname -n
printf "time="; date -Iseconds

echo "=== iostat ==="
iostat -x 1 3

cat /proc/mdstat
echo "=== dstate ==="
ps -eo pid,stat,comm,wchan:32,etime | awk '$2 ~ /^D/ {print}' | head -n 30
echo "=== diskstats ==="
cat /proc/diskstats | egrep ' (sd[a-z]|dm-[0-9]|md[0-9]) '
'

payload.message:
As of `May 3, 2026`, `edgesynology1` looks mostly healthy, while `edgesynology2` is still materially stressed.

`edgesynology1`:
- Current `iowait` is low to moderate, about `4.4%` in `top`, then `0-0.9%` in the quieter `iostat` samples.
- All arrays are healthy, including `md0/md1 [6/6]`.
- No disk is pinned. `sda` is still the weakest of the pack in the busy sample, but it was around `40.7% util`, not saturated.
- There is still one ugly long-lived kernel wait:
  - `jbd2/md0-8` stuck in `D` for about `10 days 23 hours`
- My read: usable, not in the same failure mode as `edgesynology2`, but not perfectly clean.

`edgesynology2`:
- Still under real storage pressure.
- `top` showed `12.9% iowait`.
- `iostat` showed much worse spikes in the first sample:
  - host `iowait` `21.5%`
  - `sdc` still the outlier at about `83.7% util`
  - `sdc` latency still much worse than peers: about `151 ms` read await and `216 ms` write await in that sample
  - `dm-1` / `dm-2` still heavily loaded, around `88% util`
  - `md2/md3/md4/md5` still showing elevated await, often into triple digits
- Later samples were a bit better, but still not healthy:
  - `sdc` remained the slowest disk
  - `dm-1` / `dm-2` still climbed to about `93% util`
- Arrays are still intact:
  - data arrays healthy
  - `md0/md1` still `[6/5]`, unchanged
- There is still a blocked task:
  - `synoimgbkptool` in `D` for about `1 day 6 hours`
- My read: the iSCSI ingress problem is contained, but the storage-path problem on `edgesynology2` is still present.

Bottom line:
- `edgesynology1`: not clean, but not currently distressed.
- `edgesynology2`: still stressed, and the evidence still points at the same storage-path bottleneck, with `sdc` remaining the consistent outlier.

If you want, I can next do a tighter before/after comparison on `edgesynology2` using repeated `/proc/diskstats` sampling so we can quantify exactly how much worse `sdc` is than the other four disks right now.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

content.text: is there any stress on edgesynology2 that is not related to sdc?

payload.message: is there any stress on edgesynology2 that is not related to sdc?

Yes.

`sdc` is still the strongest per-disk outlier on `edgesynology2`, but not all of the current stress can be reduced to “that one disk is bad.”

What looks broader than `sdc` alone:
- The RAID and mapper layers above it are still stressed:
  - `md2`, `md3`, `md4`, `md5` all showed elevated await
  - `dm-1` / `dm-2` were still around `88-93% util`
- There is at least one blocked userspace workload:
  - `synoimgbkptool` stuck in `D` for about `1 day 6 hours`
- System write/journal pressure still exists:
  - `md0` showed high write await in one sample
  - swap is still nonzero (`~670 MB`)
- Package/workload pressure is also present:
  - `HyperBackup`-related process was the top consumer in `D`
  - `avahi-daemon` has accumulated a lot of CPU time, though I would still treat that as secondary, not root cause

What does still look specifically tied to `sdc`:
- It remains the worst physical disk in live samples.
- Its latency is materially worse than peers even when the whole stack is busy.

So my answer is:

- `sdc` is probably the main hardware-path bottleneck.
- But the current stress on `edgesynology2` is now partly systemic above the disk:
  - RAID/device-mapper backlog
  - stuck backup/image tasks
  - filesystem/journal pressure

In other words, `sdc` is likely the trigger or dominant contributor, but it is not the only thing currently under stress.

I’m focusing on non-`sdc` contributors on `edgesynology2`: the stuck backup/image task, package activity, and any long-lived blocked work I can safely reduce before the drive swap. Then I’ll make only low-risk changes that don’t depend on the disk replacement.

{"command":"$script=@'
echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '

for p in HyperBackup SnapshotReplication ReplicationService SynologyDrive CloudSync ScsiTarget ActiveBackup-GSuite ActiveBackup-Office365 ActiveBackupForBusiness; do
  /usr/syno/bin/synopkg status "$p" 2>/dev/null || true
done

ps -ef | grep -i synoimgbkptool | grep -v grep || true
'

payload.message:
I removed the clearest non-`sdc` pressure source.

What I changed on `edgesynology2`:
- Stopped `HyperBackup`.
- Confirmed the long-stuck `synoimgbkptool` process is gone.
- That process had been running in `D` state for about `1 day 6 hours` from:
  - `/var/packages/HyperBackup/target/bin/synoimgbkptool`
  - targeting `/volume1/@img_bkp_cache/...`

What that changed:
- It did remove one real secondary stressor.
- It did not fix the core storage problem.
- After stopping `HyperBackup`, the box still shows:
  - `iowait` still around `10-21%`
  - `sdc` still the slowest disk
  - `dm-1` / `dm-2` still around `88-90% util`
  - `jbd2/md0-8` is no longer the visible blocker, but `md0_raid1` is now stuck in `D`
  - `md0` itself can still spike hard; one sample had it at `98.8% util`

So the current state is:
- `HyperBackup` was a real extra load and is now out of the way.
- The remaining stress is mostly kernel/storage-path pressure, not another easy package-level fix.
- `SynologyDrive` is still running, and I left it alone as requested.

My recommendation until the `sdc` swap:
- Keep `HyperBackup` stopped.
- Avoid starting any backup/scrub/heavy maintenance jobs.
- Leave `SynologyDrive` running if business-critical.
- Treat the remaining stress as mostly unavoidable until the disk replacement.

If you want, I can do one more cleanup pass for noncritical background services and scheduled tasks, but the gains will likely be modest compared with the disk swap.

- Avoid starting any backup/scrub/heavy maintenance jobs.
- Leave `SynologyDrive` running if business-critical.
- Treat the remaining stress as mostly unavoidable until the disk replacement.

If you want, I can do one more cleanup pass for noncritical background services and scheduled tasks, but the gains will likely be modest compared with the disk swap.

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

payload.message:
Not from what I can currently prove.

What I do see on `edgesynology2` as of `May 4, 2026`:
- `dockerd` is present, but it was only around `0.7%` CPU and `0.2%` memory.
- `containerd` was around `0.1%` CPU.
- The three `containerd-shim` processes I found were all at `0.0%` CPU and memory.
- In `top`, neither `dockerd` nor any container shim was among the dominant consumers.
- The dominant stress is still storage wait:
  - system `iowait` was about `41.8%` shortly after boot
  - later we were still seeing high `iowait`
  - package-level pressure was coming more from `HyperBackup`, `SynologyDrive`-side work, and the storage stack itself

What I could not get cleanly:
- `docker ps` and `docker stats` both hung through the Docker CLI even with the correct binary path. That means I do not have a trustworthy per-container CPU/IO table yet.

So the current answer is:
- I do not see evidence that your internal Docker containers are a primary cause of the stress.
- The runtime itself looks quiet.
- The real pressure still looks like storage-path latency and non-container Synology workloads.

The one caveat:
- Because the Docker CLI is hanging, I cannot completely rule out a container doing heavy filesystem I/O through a mounted volume. I just don’t have positive evidence for that right now.

If you want, I can do a mount-path correlation next by tracing which host paths the three running containers use and whether any of those paths line up with the hottest I/O on `volume1`.

What I do see on `edgesynology2` as of `May 4, 2026`:

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}
</app-context>

echo 'D@Mp0p123' | sudo -S sh -c '
for f in /volume1/@docker/containers/*/config.v2.json; do
  echo "=== $f mounts ==="
  grep -n "MountPoints\|Source\|Destination\|Name\|Image" "$f" | sed -n "1,120p"
  echo
 done
'
'@; $tmp=Join-Path $env:TEMP 'edgesyn2-container-config-grep.txt'; Set-Content -LiteralPath $tmp -Value $script -NoNewline; $plink='C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe'; & $plink -batch -P 1904 -pw 'D@Mp0p123' -m $tmp popdam@192.168.3.101

payload.message:
I don’t see evidence that your Docker workloads are a primary source of the stress.

What’s actually running on `edgesynology2`:
- `/synology-monitor-agent`
- `/synology-monitor-nas-api`
- `/synology-monitor-watchtower`
- `/popdam-bridge`

What their mounts look like:
- The monitor containers bind a lot of `volume1` paths, but almost all of them are read-only:
  - `/volume1/files`
  - `/volume1/users`
  - `/volume1/mgmt`
  - `/volume1/styleguides`
  - `/volume1/mac`
  - plus some other shares and Synology metadata dirs
- The main writable Docker path I found is:
  - `/volume1/@docker/volumes/synology-monitor-agent_agent-data/_data`
- `watchtower` only has `/var/run/docker.sock` and a broad `/volume1` bind.
- `popdam-bridge` only reads:
  - `/volume1/mac`
  - `/volume1/styleguides`

What that suggests:
- These containers are mostly observers/readers, not heavy writers.
- I did not find a container with an obvious large writable bind into the hot shares.
- The runtime-side processes (`dockerd`, `containerd`, shims) were also quiet in the earlier process sample.

One notable detail:
- `synology-monitor-nas-api` had repeated health check timeouts on May 4, 2026.
- That looks more like the container is suffering from the NAS being slow than causing the slowness.

So my read is:
- Docker is touching the stressed storage, yes.
- But I do not see undue Docker-generated load that explains the current I/O problem.
- The monitor stack may amplify read pressure a bit because it scans shares, but it does not look like the dominant issue next to the storage-path latency already present on the host.

If you want, I can next quantify that “a bit” by temporarily stopping just the two monitoring containers for a minute and comparing `iostat` before/after. That would tell us whether they are contributing materially or just incidentally.
